question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$ can be distinguished by haloform test.

Reason: Haloform test is given by $2^{0}$ alcohol.
explanation:
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{OH}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{I}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{/O}}{{\text{H}}^\Theta }}}\mathop {{\text{CH}}{{\text{I}}_{\text{3}}}}\limits_{{\text{ Yellow ppt}}{\text{. }}} $
The above test is iodoform test. It is given by $\alpha$-methyl ketones, aldehydes. It can also be given by $1^{\circ}, 2^{\circ}$ alcohols, if they have $\beta$-methylgroup. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954889/preptracker/questions/q_28483_explanation_1764954889410.jpg can give iodoform test but not by https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954890/preptracker/questions/q_28483_explanation_1764954890021.jpg (Both these are $2^{\circ}$ alcohols) So. all $2^{\circ}$ alcohols do not give this test.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Vinylic alcohols contain an $ - {\text{OH}}$ group bonded to a $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized carbon atom.
Alcohols can be categorized based on the hybridization state of the carbon atom to which the $ - {\text{OH}}$ group is attached.
explanation: Statement A is false because vinylic alcohols contain an  $ - {\text{OH}}$  group bonded to an $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridized carbon atom of an alkene, not $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized carbon atom. Statement B is true; alcohols are indeed categorized based on the hybridization of the carbon to which the  $ - {\text{OH}}$  group is attached (e.g., $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ for aliphatic alcohols, $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ for vinylic alcohols). Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subtopic: Classification of Alcohols

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Allylic alcohols have the hydroxyl group attached to the carbon atom of a double bond.
Benzylic alcohols have the hydroxyl group attached to a $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized carbon atom next to aromatic system.
explanation: Statement  A  is false because allylic alcohols have the hydroxyl group attached to a carbon atom that is adjacent to another carbon-carbon double bond, not to the double bond itself. Statement B is true; benzylic alcohols have the hydroxyl group attached to a $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized carbon atom next to an aromatic system. Correct option is (3)

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Ethers are classified based on the similarity of alkyl or aryl groups attached to the oxygen atom.
Simple or symmetrical ethers have identical alkyl or aryl groups attached to the oxygen atom.
explanation: Ethers are classified as simple (symmetrical) if both alkyl or aryl groups attached to the oxygen atom are identical and as mixed (unsymmetrical) if they are different. Therefore, both statements are true. Correct option is (1)

A secondary alcohol has two hydroxyl groups attached to the same carbon atom.

question:
Reactions that produce n -butanol in the following are :
(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} \xrightarrow[\text { (ii) } \mathrm{NaOH} / \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}]{\text { (i) } \mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}}$
(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO} \xrightarrow[\text { (ii) } \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}]{\text { (i) } \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Mgl}}$
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CN} \xrightarrow[\substack{\text { (ii) } \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+} \\ \text {(iii) } \mathrm{NaBH}_{4}}]{\text { (i) } \mathrm{SnCl}_{2}, \mathrm{HCl}}$
(IV) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{H} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}}$
explanation:
I. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} \xrightarrow[\substack{\text { (ii) NaOH } / \mathrm{O}_{2} \\ \text { (Hydroboration oxidation) }}]{\text { (i) } \mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}}$ $\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{OH}}}\limits_{{\text{n}}\,{\text{ - }}\,{\text{butanol}}} $
II. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO} \xrightarrow[\text { (ii) } \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}]{\text { (i) } \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgI}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952378/preptracker/questions/q_108197_explanation_1764952377732.png
III. ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{CN}}\xrightarrow[\substack{ {\text{ (i) }}{{\text{H}}_3}{3^ \oplus } \\ {\text{ (Stephen reduction) }} } ]{{{\text{ (i) SnC}}{{\text{l}}_2},{\text{HCl}}}}$ ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{CHO}}\xrightarrow[{[{\text{H}}]}]{{{\text{LiAl}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}}}$ $\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{OH}}}\limits_{{\text{n - butanol}}} $
IV. ${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{COOH}}\xrightarrow[{[{\text{H}}]}]{{{{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}}}$ $\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{OH}}}\limits_{{\text{n - butanol}}} $
So, reactions I, III and IV produce n - butanol.

question:
The acid-catalyzed hydration of alkenes involves the following three steps:
I. Nucleophilic attack of water on carbocation.
II. Protonation of alkene to form carbocation by the electrophilic attack of $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$.
III. Deprotonation to form an alcohol.
Identify the sequence for the mechanism of reaction in the acid-catalyzed hydration of alkenes.
explanation:
The reaction of an alkene with water in the presence of acid gives alcohol. This reaction involves three steps in the following sequence:
(I) Protonation of alkene to form carbocation by the electrophilic attack of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}}}$.
(II) Nucleophilic attack of water on carbocation.
(III) Deprotonation to form an alcohol.

question:
The acid catalysed hydration of alkene involves the following three steps.
I. Nucleophilic attack of water on carbocation.

III. Deprotonation to form an alcohol.
Identify the sequence for the mechanism of reation in the acid catalysed hydration of alkenes.

question:
Decreasing order of reactivity of the following compounds in the Williamson's ether synthesis is
(i) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952351/preptracker/questions/q_108174_question_1764952351154.png
(ii) ${\text{Cl}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{CH}} = {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}$
(iii) ${\text{Cl}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}} = {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}$
(iv) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952352/preptracker/questions/q_108174_question_1764952351780.png
explanation:
Williamson's ether synthesis takes place via $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ mechanism. So, alkyl chloride with more steric hindrance will be less reactive in nature. Out of (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv), (i) will have high steric hindrance and will be least reactive. Alkyl chloride (ii) reacts faster than (iii) and (iv). Since $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-$ is electron withdrawing which makes $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ more electron deficient than (iii)and (iv). In other words, nucleophilic attack occur faster on (ii). Out of (iii) and (iv), (iii) will be more electron deficient than (iv) due to absence of one methyl group, i.e. electrondonating group.
Hence, correct order of reactivity towards Williamson's ether synthesis will be (ii) $>$ (iii) $>$ (iv) $>$ (i)
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Williamson’s Synthesis

question: The reaction used for the synthesis of ether is
explanation: Williamson's synthesis is  $\mathrm{RO}^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{RX} \rightarrow \underset{\text { (ether) }}{\mathrm{ROR}}+\mathrm{NaX}$.

question:
How many of the following ethers CANNOT be prepared by Williamson's synthesis?
${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}, \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OCH}_{3},\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
${\left( {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)_{\text{3}}}{\text{COC}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{,}}{\left( {{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}{\text{O,}}$
${\left( {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)_{\text{3}}}{\text{C - O - C}}{\left( {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)_{\text{3}}}$
explanation:
In Williamson's synthesis,
i. ${\mathrm{ 1^{\circ}}}$ alkyl halide and ${\mathrm{3^{\circ}}}$ alkoxide give best yield of corresponding ethers.
ii. In case of, ${\mathrm{3^{\circ}}}$ alkyl halide, elimination competes over substitution and alkene is obtained as a product.
iii. Aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution unless it is activated by the presence of electron withdrawing group.
Thus, ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ cannot be prepared by Williamson's synthesis.

$\xrightarrow{{{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{Br}}}}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5} - {\text{O}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{{\text{ Methyl phenylether }}} $

The Lucas test distinguishes alcohols by the rate at which they form alkyl halides with hydrogen halides.
Primary alcohols show immediate turbidity in the Lucas test due to the rapid formation of alkyl halides.
explanation: The Lucas test does distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols based on the rate of formation of alkyl halides. explanation:
a) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955046/preptracker/questions/q_28918_explanation_1764955046095.jpg b) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955047/preptracker/questions/q_28918_explanation_1764955047060.jpg c) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955049/preptracker/questions/q_28918_explanation_1764955048597.jpg Primary, Secondary and Tertiary alcohols can be distinguished by Victor Meyer's method.
Primary alcohol gives red colour, secondary gives blue colour and tertiary alcohol has no reaction. So the option (1) is correct.

question: Which of the following compounds reacts immediately with Lucas reagent?
explanation:
Lucas reagent is used to distinguish primary, secondary or tertiary alcohols. Alcohols are soluble in Lucas reagent while their halides are immiscible and produce turbidity in solution.
In case of tertiary alcohols, turbidity is produced immediately as they form the halides easily. Primary alcohols do not produce turbidity at room temperature.

question: Lucas test is associated with
explanation:
Lucas test is employed to distinguish between $$1^{0}, 2^{0}$$ and $$3^{0}$$ alcohols. Lucas reagent, i.e., conc. $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ and anhyd. $$\mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}$$, is added to alcohols and the appearance of turbidity due to formation of alkyl chloride helps in the distinction of alcohols.
If turbidity appears immediately, alcohol is tertiary. If turbidity appears in $$5 \mathrm{~min}$$, alcohol is secondary. If solution remains clear, i.e., no turbidity, alcohol is primary.

question: Match the following reactions in Column I with the corresponding products in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
Acid catalysed hydration (A) is a method to prepare alcohols (Q) from alkenes where the hydroxyl group gets attached to the more substituted carbon atom according to Markovnikov's rule.
Hydroboration-oxidation (B) also converts alkenes to alcohols (P), but it follows the anti - Markovnikov's rule, adding the hydroxyl group to the less substituted carbon atom.
Williamson synthesis (C) is a reaction that forms ethers (S) by reacting an alkyl halide with an alkoxide ion.
The oxidation of cumene (D) is an industrial process used for the simultaneous production of phenol and acetone (R). Correct option is (1)

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below
p-Nitrophenol is more acidic than m-nitrophenol and o-nitrophenol.
Ethanol will give immediate turbidity with Lucas reagent.
explanation: ${\mathrm{p}}$-Nitrophenol is more acidic than meta and ortho-isomers. The electron withdrawing group, ${\mathrm{-\mathrm{NO}_{2}}}$ stabilises the phenoxide ion through $-$R effect at the ortho and para position. The effective delocalisation of negative charge in phenoxide ion enhance the acidic strength of phenols. The ${\mathrm{-\mathrm{NO}_{2}}}$ group at meta position will show $-$I effect only. The ${\mathrm{-\mathrm{NO}_{2}}}$ group at ortho position forms hydrogen bond with $-$OH group. Ethanol being primary alcohol does not react with Lucas reagent at room temperature. Tertiary alcohol will give turbidity immediately with Lucas reagent. So, correct option is (1).

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
Assertion: Phenol is more acidic than ethanol.

Reason: Phenoxide ion is resonance stabilised.
explanation: Phenol's higher acidity than ethanol is attributed to the electronegative oxygen in the phenolic - $\mathrm{OH}$ group, which stabilizes the resulting negative charge through resonance over the aromatic ring. This facilitates proton donation. Ethanol lacks such resonance stabilization. So the option (1) is correct.

explanation: Sodium drying is a process involving the use of sodium metal to remove moisture from non-polar solvents like diethyl ether and benzene by selectively reacting with traces of water. In polar solvents like ethanol, a more vigorous reaction occurs forming sodium ethoxide and hydrogen gas So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Alcohols are easily protonated as compared to phenols.


Reason: Alcohols undergo intermolecular hydrogen bonding due to the presence of highly electronegative oxygen.
explanation: The resonance effect in phenols disperses electron density over the benzene ring, reducing availability for protonation. This explains their lower acidity as compared to alcohols with localized lone pairs that readily react with acids. So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: The ionisation constant of phenol is higher than ethanol.


Reason: Phenoxide ion is stabilised through delocalisation.
explanation: The hydroxyl group, in phenol is directly attached to the $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridised carbon of benzene ring which acts as an electron withdrawing group. The resonance in phenol causes the oxygen of -OH group to be positive. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955018/preptracker/questions/q_28842_explanation_1764955017844.png  This increases the polarity of $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{H}$ bond and results in an increase in ionisation constant of phenols. So the option (1) is correct.

question: Match the IUPAC names of ethers in Column I with their corresponding common names in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
Methoxymethane $(\mathrm{A})$ is another name for Dimethyl ether (Q), which is the common name for an ether where both alkyl groups are methyl groups.
Ethoxyethane (B) is the IUPAC name for Diethyl ether (P), which is the common name for an ether where both alkyl groups are ethyl groups.
Methoxybenzene (C) is the IUPAC name for Anisole (S), which is the common name for an ether where the alkyl group is a methyl group attached to a benzene ring.
Ethoxybenzene (D) is the IUPAC name for Phenetole (R), which is the common name for an ether where the alkyl group is an ethyl group attached to a benzene ring. Correct option is (1)

options.text: A - Q,       	B - P, 	C - S, 	D - R
A - Q, 	B - P,	C - R, 	D - S

A - S,  	B - R,	C - Q, 	D - P

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
In phenols, the $ - {\text{OH}}$ group is attached to a ${\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{2}}}$ hybridized carbon of the aromatic ring.
The carbon-oxygen bond length in phenols is greater than that in alcohols due to delocalization.
explanation: Statement A is true because the  $ - {\text{OH}}$  group in phenols is indeed attached to an $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridized carbon atom of the aromatic ring. Statement B is false because the carbon oxygen bond length in phenols is actually slightly less than that in alcohols due to the partial double bond character caused by the resonance of the lone pair of electrons on the oxygen with the aromatic ring. Correct option is (2)

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The IUPAC name of tertiary butyl alcohol is 2-Methylpropan-2-ol.

Reason: In IUPAC nomenclature, the longest carbon chain is chosen as the parent chain and numbered to give the substituents the lowest possible numbers.
explanation: The IUPAC name of tertiary butyl alcohol is indeed 2-Methylpropan-2-ol, as it consists of a methyl group on the second carbon of propane, which is also where the hydroxyl group is located. However, the reason provided is a general rule for naming but not the specific reason why the name is 2-Methylpropan-2-ol, as it does not pertain to the substituents but to the placement of the functional group. Correct option is (2)

question: Match the properties of alcohols in Column I with their related explanations in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
Alcohols have higher boiling points (A) due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding (Q).
The solubility of alcohols in water (B) is explained by their ability to form hydrogen bonds with water molecules (P).
Increased branching leads to decreased boiling points (C) because of a decrease in van der Waals forces with decreased surface area (S).
Alcohols have a higher boiling point than hydrocarbons of comparable molecular mass (D) due to the presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonds (R), which are absent in hydrocarbons. Correct option is (1)

explanation: Ethers do generally have higher boiling points than hydrocarbons of similar molecular  weight due to their dipole moment, although they do not have as high a boiling point as alcohols due to the absence of hydrogen bonding. Correct option is (3)

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Ethers cannot form intermolecular hydrogen bonds like alcohols, which results in lower boiling points.
Ethers are more soluble in water than hydrocarbons of comparable molecular masses because they can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules.
explanation: The inability of ethers to form hydrogen bonds among themselves like alcohols results in their lower boiling points. However, the oxygen atom in ethers can form hydrogen bonds with water, making them relatively soluble in water. Correct option is (1)

question: How many of the following compounds will have higher boiling point than butan-2-ol?
explanation:
Factors affecting boiling point of alcohols:
i. The boiling points of alcohols are higher than the corresponding alkanes, aldehydes, ketones, ethers, etc., of nearly same molecular masses due to the presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
ii. The boiling point of alcohol increases with increase in molecular mass.
iii. The boiling point of alcohol decreases with increase in branching.
Thus pentan-1-ol (II), butan-1-ol (VI) and hexan-1-ol (VII) will have higher boiling point as compared to butan-2-ol.
So, the correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: Methyl alcohol is more volatile than water.


Reason: The oxygen containing angle is maximum in water than methanol.
explanation: In the case of methanol $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}\right)$ and water $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$,the oxygen atom is $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955025/preptracker/questions/q_28880_explanation_1764955025310.png  The larger size of the $\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group in methanol compared to a single hydrogen atom results in greater steric hindrance. While there are lone pair-lone pair repulsions, these are outweighed by the steric hindrance from the larger $\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group. As a result, the angle between the oxygen atom and other atoms in methanol is greater compared to that in water. So the option (3) is correct.

explanation: Among the given compounds, hydroxybenzene (IV) has least molar mass and therefore possess least boiling. 1, 2dihydroxybenzene (I) forms intramolecular $$\mathrm{H}$$- bonding with result that it will not form intermolecular H-bonding leading to lowest boiling point. On the other hand 1, 3dihydroxybenzene (II) and 1, 4dihydroxybenzene (III) does not undergo chelation, hence they will involve extensive intermolecular $$\mathrm{H}$$-bonding leading to higher boiling point. Further intermolecular hydrogen bonding is stronger in the p-isomer than the $$\mathrm{m}$$- isomer hence former has highest b.p. Thus the decreasing order of boiling points is III $$>$$ II $$>$$ I $$>$$ IV.

question: Which of the following cannot be used to oxidise  primary alcohols to aldehydes?
explanation:
When one of the alkyl groups is tertiary, the alkyl halide is formed from the tertiary alkyl group.https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_07_19_44bcea5c651c916ea4a9g-02.jpg?height=204&width=461&top_left_y=669&top_left_x=1254 tert - Butylmethyl ether https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958353/preptracker/questions/q_108308_explanation_1764958353162.png
tert-Butyl iodide Methyl alcohol.
This is due to the reason that the reaction occurs by $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism and the formation of products is controlled by the stability of the carbocation resulting from the cleavage of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ bond in protonated ether (oxonium ion). Since a tert-butyl carbocation is more stable than methyl carbocation is more stable than methyl carbonation, therefore, cleavage of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ bond gives methyl alcohol and the more stable tert$ - $butyl carbocation. This carbocation then reacts with $\mathrm{I}^{-}$ ion to form tert-butyl iodide.

question: 0.218 g of acetyl derivative of polyhydric alcohol with molecular mass ${\mathrm{=92}}$, requires 0.168 g of KOH for hydrolysis. The number of ${\mathrm{(-\mathrm{OH})}}$ groups in the alcohol being halved is ____ .
explanation:
Let formula of Alcohol ${\mathrm{=\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{OH})_{n} \&}}$ acyl derivative ${\mathrm{=\mathrm{R}\left[\mathrm{OCOCH}_{3}\right]_{n}}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958872/preptracker/questions/q_29714_explanation_1764958872234.png Molecular mass of $\left. {{\rm{R[}} - {\rm{O}} - {\rm{COC}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right]\,\,({\rm{m}}\,{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{42n}})$ Molecular mass of Alcohol ${\mathrm{=\mathrm{M}}}$
Molecular mass of ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CO}-\right)}}$ group ${\mathrm{=43}}$
${\mathrm{1-\mathrm{OH}}}$ group of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}}}$ replaces 1-H atom.
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Mol. mass of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{OCOCH}_{3}\right)=[m+(43-1) n]}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{ = [m}}\,{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{42n]}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{OCOCH}_{3}\right) \xrightarrow{n \mathrm{KOH}} \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{OH})_{n}+n \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}}}$
Mol. mass of ${\mathrm{n \mathrm{KOH}=65 n}}$
0.218 g of acetyl derivative requires 0.168 g KOH ${\mathrm{(m+42 n) \mathrm{g}}}$ acetyl derivative requires
$=\dfrac{0.168(m+42 n)}{0.218}=56 n$
${\rm{30}}{\rm{.67}}\,{\rm{n = 92}}\,$
${\rm{n = 2}}{\rm{.9 = 3}}$
${\rm{No}}{\rm{.of}} - {\rm{OH}}\,{\rm{groups = 3}}$

question: $0.336\,{\rm{g}}\,\,{\rm{KOH}}$ hydrolyses with $0.436 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetyl derivative of polyhydric alcohol. (molecular mass $=92$ ). Then the number of hydroxyl groups in the alcohol are ____.
explanation:
We know that, $\frac{{\rm{w}}}{{\rm{W}}}({\rm{M}} + 42\,{\rm{n}}) = 56\,{\rm{n}}$
Where $\mathrm{w}$ is the amount of alkali, $\mathrm{W}$ is amount of acetyl derivative, M is the molecular mass of alcohol and n is the number of hydroxyl groups.
$\frac{{0.336}}{{0.436}}(92 + 42\,{\rm{n}}) = 56\,{\rm{n}}$
$92 + 42{\rm{n}} = \frac{{0.436}}{{0.336}} \times 56{\rm{n}} = \frac{{218}}{3}{\rm{n}}$
or $276+126 \mathrm{n}=218 \mathrm{n}$
or $\mathrm{n}=3$ (the alcohol is trihydric.)

question:
At 413 K , ethanol is dehydrated to produce ethoxyethane.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow[413 \mathrm{~K}]{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$
Read the statements given below, and choose the option containing the correct statements.
I. Such a reaction is suitable for the preparation of ethers having primary alkyl groups only.
II. The mechanism involves the following sequence Protonation of alcohol $\rightarrow$ Attack of another alcohol molecule on protonated alcohol $\rightarrow$ Deprotonation.
III. At 443 K , the same reaction would produce ethene as the major product.
explanation:
Alkene and ether can be formed by dehydration of alcohol, that depends on reaction condition. At 443 K , dehydration of alcohol will give alkene whereas at 413 K the same reaction will give ether.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949359/preptracker/questions/q_29197_explanation_1764949359078.png
This reaction follows ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2}}$ reaction pathway so primary alkyl halide is suitable for preparation of ether.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Elimination Reaction: Dehydration Reactions

question: 0.092 g of a compound with the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{8} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ on reaction with an excess $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgI}$ gives 67.00 mL of methane at STP. The number of active hydrogen atoms present in a molecule of the compound is
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{ROH}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgI} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{OR}) \mathrm{I}}}$
According to the above reaction, every one mole of a compound containing one active H atom per molecule releases one mole of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}}$ gas at STP. Thus, number of moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}}$ gas released per mole of compound should be an indicator of the number of active hydrogen atoms per molecule of compound.
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}=\dfrac{\text { Vol. of } \mathrm{CH}_{4} \text { at STP }}{22,400 \mathrm{~mL} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}=0.003 \mathrm{~mol}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{n}_{\text {compound }}=\dfrac{\text { Given mass }}{\text { Molar mass }}}}$
${\mathrm{=\dfrac{0.092 \mathrm{~g}}{92 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}=0.001 \mathrm{~mol}}}$
This implies that 1 mol of compound releases 3 moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CH}_{4}}}$ gas i.e., there are three active hydrogen atoms present in a molecule of the compound.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Miscellaneous Questions on Preparation and Reactions of Alcohols

question: The relative rate of acid catalyzed dehydration of following alcohols would be
explanation:
Rate of dehydration $ \propto $ stability of first formed carbocation.
Carbocation formed from (III)….stabilizes through resonance and hyper conjugation.
Carbocation formed from (I)….stabilizes through hyper conjugation and inductive effect.
Carbocations (II) and (IV) are primary but stability of (IV) is more eventhough it has less number of H.C hydrogen because it has strong +1 group.
So, the correct option is (3).

question:
Which of the following statements are correct regarding esterification of alcohols?
I. Alcohols react with carboxylic acids, acid chlorides, and acid anhydrides to form esters.
II. The reaction with carboxylic acid and acid anhydride is carried out in presence of a small amount of conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
III. The reaction with acid chloride is carried out in presence of a base (pyridine) to neutralize the side product HCl .
IV. The reaction with carboxylic acid and acid anhydride is reversible while with acid chloride, it is irreversible.
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{RO}-\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{COOH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}^{+}} \mathrm{RCOOR}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{RO}-\mathrm{H}+\mathrm{RCOCl} \xrightarrow{\text { Pyridine }} \mathrm{RCOOR}+\mathrm{HCl}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{RO}-\mathrm{H}+\left(\mathrm{RCO}_{2}\right) \mathrm{O} \stackrel{\mathrm{H}^{+}}{\rightleftharpoons} \mathrm{RCOOR}+\mathrm{RCOOH}}}$
The reaction of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}}}$ with acid chloride is irreversible, so to shift the equilibrium to the right hand side, pyridine is used which removes HCl from the reaction mixture.

explanation:

(ii) Since alcohol and phenol are acidic in nature, they react with active metal to produce corresponding alkoxide and phenoxide.
(iii) Alcohol is weak acid, so in the presence of strong Bronsted acid, it acts as base (not as an acid).

question:
Assertion: Addition of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ to phenyl magnesium bromide gives benzene.


Reason: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ is a stronger acid than benzene.
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955027/preptracker/questions/q_28903_explanation_1764955026847.png   Methanol acts an acid and donates its proton to phenyl magnesium bromide which acts as a base and accepts the proton, to produce benzene. Methanol is a stronger acid than benzene as benzene is resonance stabilized. So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ is a nucleophile.


Reason: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ forms sodium methoxide on reaction with $\mathrm{NaOH}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{CO}^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Methanol is acidic, The presence of the hydroxy group $(-\mathrm{OH})$ in this molecule means that it can donate a pair of electrons from the oxygen atom. This makes it a potential site for chemical reactions where it can form new bonds by sharing its electron pair with other atoms or molecules, thus functioning as a nucleophile. So the option (2) is correct

question:
Assertion: tert-Butoxide is a stronger base than $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$or $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}^{-}$but is a much poorer nucleophile.


Reason: A negatively charged ion is always a more powerful nucleophile than its conjugate acid.
explanation: Steric hindrance in the tert-butoxide ion, owing to its larger size compared to smaller alkoxide ions, significantly impedes the attack on a carbon atom in nucleophilic substitution reactions. So the option (2) is correct.

Assertion: An alcohol does not react with halide ions as such but on dissolution in strong acids it does react smoothly forming an alkyl halide.


Reason: The strong acid accelerates the removal of proton from the -OH group of the alcohol.
explanation: Protonation of the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group by strong acids converts it into a better leaving group, crucial for facilitating nucleophilic substitution reactions by forming a stable water molecule as the leaving group. So the option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Alcohols turn blue litmus red.

Reason: Alcohols give red colour with ceric ammonium nitrate solution.
explanation: Alcohols are considered weak acids and, as a result, they do not exhibit the typical acidic behavior of turning blue litmus paper red. Ceric ammonium nitrate test serves as an analytical method for identifying alcohols. In this test alcohols react with the reagent to produce a distinctive red coloration. So the option (4) is correct.

explanation: The acidity of alcohols depends on the $+\mathrm{I}$ effect of alkyl groups, making tertiary alcohols less acidic due to stronger electron-releasing effects than primary alcohols. So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ undergoes esterification faster than $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$.


Reason: The reaction between an acid and alcohol in presence of dry $\mathrm{HCl}$ gas to give ester is known as esterification process.
explanation: During esterification, when the size of the alkyl group in either the acid or the alcohol increases, the rate of esterification tends to decrease. This is due to steric hindrance. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$ has a smaller $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group which offers less steric hindrance. Whereas, $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$ has a bulky t-butyl group which offers more steric hindrance. So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: The hydrolysis of ethyl acctate is catalysed by mineral acid.


Reason: Esterification takes place between ethyl alcohol and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ at $170^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
explanation:
Hydrolysis of ethyl acetate is acid catalysed
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}^{+}}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$
Excess concentrated sulfuric acid at elevated temperatures catalyzes the dehydration of alcohols, resulting in the elimination of water and the formation of alkenes.
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow[170^{\circ} \mathrm{C}]{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
So the option (3) is correct.

question: $$0.037 \mathrm{~g}$$ of an alcohol, $$\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}$$ was added to $$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgBr}$$ and the gas evolved measured 11.2 $$\mathrm{mL}$$ at STP. The molecular mass of $$\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}$$ will be
explanation:
$\mathop {{\rm{R - OH}}}\limits_{{\rm{1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{mol}}} {\rm{ + C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{MgBr}} \to \mathop {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{4}}}}\limits_{\scriptstyle{\rm{1 mol }}\atop {\scriptstyle{\rm{ or }}\atop \scriptstyle{\rm{22400}}\,{\rm{mL at STP}}}} {\rm{ + Mg(Br)OR}}$
$11.2\,{\text{mL C}}{{\text{H}}_4}$ at STP is formed from
$$0.037 \mathrm{~g}$$ $$\mathrm{ROH}$$.
$\therefore 22400\,{\text{mL C}}{{\text{H}}_4}$ at STP will be formed from $\frac{{{\text{0}}{\text{.037}}}}{{{\text{11}}{\text{.2}}}} \times {\text{22400 g ROH = 74 g ROH}}$
$$\therefore \quad$$ Molecular mass of $$\mathrm{ROH}=74$$

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The methoxy group in anisole directs electrophilic substitution to the ortho and para positions.
Anisole does not react with bromine water to form brominated products.
explanation: The methoxy group is an activating group and directs electrophilic substitution to the ortho and para positions due to resonance stabilization. Anisole readily reacts with bromine water to form brominated products, typically without the need for a catalyst. Correct option is (2)

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
In the presence of HI, ethers typically undergo a $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reaction leading to the formation of alkyl iodides.
The reaction with HI proceeds through the formation of an oxonium ion intermediate.
explanation: The reaction of ethers with HI proceeds through the formation of an oxonium ion, followed by an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ mechanism where the alkoxide group is a good leaving group. The $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism is not typical with ethers unless tertiary groups are present. Correct option is (3)

The methoxy group is a meta directing group in electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.

question: Which reaction is used in Zeisel's method of detection and estimation of alkoxy group?
explanation: In Zeisel's method the compound is heated with excess HI, forming an alcohol and iodomethane. The iodomethane is distilled off and added into an acidic solution of silver nitrate where it precipitates silver iodide which is then filtered, weighed and the number of iodine atoms as well as the number of methoxy groups can be calculated.

question:
Assertion: Etherates are coordination complexes of ethers with Lewis acids.


Reason: Ethers are easily cleaved by mineral acids such as $\mathrm{HCl}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ at $373 \mathrm{~K}$.
explanation: Ethers, act as Lewis bases. Ethers, with their lone pairs of electrons on the oxygen atom, can readily donate electron pairs to Lewis acids. When they interact with Lewis acids, the resulting complexes are commonly referred to as "ethers" or "etherates." Ethers are not easily cleaved by sulphuric acid or $\mathrm{HCl}$. Thus assertion is true but reason is false. So the option (3) is correct.

So the option (3) is correct.

question:
In the presence of light, perchlorodiethyl ether is obtained from diethyl ether.
$\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{O} \stackrel{\mathrm{Cl}_{2}}{\longrightarrow}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{O}$
The mechanism through which this reaction proceed is
explanation:
The given reaction is,
${\left( {{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\left( {{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{5}}}} \right)_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
The reaction proceeds by a free - radical mechanism and $\alpha$ - substitution occurs readily because of resonance stabilisation of the intermediate.
${\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{h}}\upsilon }}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ \bullet }{\text{ + C}}{{\text{l}}^ \bullet }$
${\text{R}}\mathop {\mathop {\text{O}}\limits_{ \bullet \bullet } }\limits^{ \bullet \bullet } {\text{ - C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{R'}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ \bullet }}}{\text{R}}\mathop {\mathop {\text{O}}\limits_{ \bullet \bullet } }\limits^{ \bullet \bullet } {\text{ - }}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ \bullet {\text{HR' + HCl}}$
${\text{R}}\mathop {\mathop {\text{O}}\limits_{ \bullet \bullet } }\limits^{ \bullet \bullet } {\text{ - }}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ \bullet {\text{HR'}} \leftrightarrow {\text{R}}\mathop {\mathop {\text{O}}\limits_{ \bullet \bullet } }\limits^ \bullet {\text{ = CHR'}}$
${\text{R}}\mathop {\mathop {\text{O}}\limits_{ \bullet \bullet } }\limits^{ \bullet \bullet } {\text{ - }}\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^ \bullet {\text{HR'}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{R}}\mathop {\mathop {\text{O}}\limits_{ \bullet \bullet } }\limits^{ \bullet \bullet } {\text{ - CHClR' + C}}{{\text{l}}^ \bullet }$

question: $$\mathrm{HBr}$$ reacts with $$\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$$ under anhydrous conditions at room temperature to give:
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{CH}_{2} & =\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}+\mathrm{Br}^{-} \\& \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{Br}) \mathrm{OCH}_{3} .\end{aligned}$$
The formed Carbocation, $$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$$ is stabilised by resonance with oxygen so its formation is more favourable than $$\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OCH}_{3}$$.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Phenols can form sodium phenoxide upon treatment with sodium hydroxide due to their acidic nature.
Phenols cannot undergo electrophilic substitution reactions because the $ - {\text{OH}}$ group deactivates the benzene ring.
explanation: Phenols are acidic enough to react with bases like sodium hydroxide to form sodium phenoxide. However, Statement B is incorrect because the  $ - {\text{OH}}$  group in phenols actually activates the benzene ring towards electrophilic  substitution reactions and directs the incoming electrophiles at the ortho and para positions due to resonance stabilization of the phenoxide ion formed during the reaction. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Arrange the following phenols in decreasing order of their $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$.
(A) Phenol
(B) ortho - nitrophenol
(C) meta - nitrophenol
(D) para - nitrophenol
explanation:
$\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ value is inversely proportional to the acidity, i.e. the smaller the $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ value, the stronger is the acid. $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group at ortho and para-position withdraws electron of $ - {\text{OH}}$ group towards itself due to their $ - {\text{R}}$ effect while $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group at meta-position withdraw electron from $ - {\text{OH}}$ group by the weaker $ - {\text{I}}$ effect. Thus, ortho and para - nitrophenols are more acidic than meta nitrophenol. Also, para - nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho - nitrophenol due to intermolecular ${\text{H}} - $ bonding in ortho - nitrophenol which makes loss of proton little more difficult. Phenol is least acidic in nature due to absence of electron - withdrawing group. So, acidity order will be $\mathop {{\text{para}} - {\text{nitrophenol}}}\limits_{({\text{D}})} > \mathop {{\text{ortho}} - {\text{nitrophenol}}}\limits_{({\text{B}})} $ $ > \mathop {{\text{meta}} - {\text{nitrophenol}}}\limits_{({\text{C}})} > \mathop {{\text{phenol}}}\limits_{({\text{A}})} $
Hence, the correct $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ value order will be A $>$ C $>$ B $>$ D
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Acidic Nature and Effect of Substituents on the Acidity of Phenols

question: Find the correct order of acid strengths of the following compounds:
explanation:
In case (F) para nitrophenol $ - {\text{R}}$ effect of $\left(-\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)$ group at para position of benzene ring increase its acidity by electron withdrawing effect of $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group.
In case (D) para hydroxy acetophenone $-\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$ group shows less $(-\mathrm{R})$ effect than $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group. So, it will be less acidic than para nitrophenol.
In case (E), m-nitrophenol will show less acidic character than D because in this case only $ - {\text{I}}$ effect is present.
In case (C) $ - {\text{OMe}}$ is a electron releasing group which decrease the stability of phenoxide ion by increasing electron density so it will be less acidic than (B) and in case of (A) resonance is not present. Hence, it will be less acidic than C .
Hence, the correct order of acidic strengths of acid is $\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{D}>\mathrm{E}>\mathrm{B}>\mathrm{C}>\mathrm{A}$.

explanation:

question:
The increasing order of $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ for the following phenols is
(A) 2, 4 – Dinitrophenol
(B) 4 – Nitrophenol
(C) 2, 4, 5 – Trimethylphenol
(D) Phenol
(E) 3 – Chlorophenol
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
Higher the stability of resulting phenoxide ion, lower will be the $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$. Electron withdrawing groups stabilise the phenoxide ion and electron releasing groups destablise the phenoxide ion. Nitro and chloro groups increase acidic nature and decrease pKa value. Methyl group decreases acidic nature and increases pKa value.Hence, the correct order of increasing pKa values is,
(A) $ < $ (B) $ < $ (E) $ < $ (D) $ < $ (C).

explanation: Phenol forms 2,4,6-Tribromophenol (white $\mathrm{ppt})$ when treated with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ in water. In phenols, the polarization of the bromine molecule occurs even in the absence of a Lewis acid. This is because phenols are more reactive than benzene due to the presence of the hydroxyl ($\mathrm{-OH}$ ) group, which activates the ring and makes it more susceptible to electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955085/preptracker/questions/q_28999_explanation_1764955084899.png  So the option (2) is correct.

explanation: Phenol is a stronger acid than ethanol due to the resonance stabilization of the phenoxide ion, resulting in increased stability compared to the ethoxide ion formed from ethanol. question:
Assertion: Phenol is more reactive than benzene towards electrophilic substitution reaction.


Reason: In the case of phenol, the intermediate carbocation is more resonance stabilised.
explanation: The $-\mathrm{OH}$ group, attached to the benzene ring, in phenol activates it towards electrophilic substitution and directs the incoming group to the ortho and para positions as these positions become electron rich due to the resonance. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955074/preptracker/questions/q_28979_explanation_1764955074369.png  In contrast, benzene lacks the electron-donating $-\mathrm{OH}$ group. As a result, electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions are less efficient and slower in benzene compared to phenol. So the option (1) is correct.

Reason: In water, ionisation of phenol is enhanced but in $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$, it is greatly suppressed.
explanation: In aqueous solution phenol ionizes to give phenoxide ion. The ring is slightly activated and hence mono substitution occurs. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955045/preptracker/questions/q_28910_explanation_1764955045106.png  So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Treatment of phenol with nitrous acid yields p-benzoquinone monoxime.


Reason: p-Nitrosophenol and p-benzoquinone monoxime are tautomers.
explanation:
When phenol, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955043/preptracker/questions/q_28906_explanation_1764955042865.png reacts with nitrous acid $\left(\mathrm{HNO}_{2}\right)$, the hydroxyl group (-OH) on phenol is replaced by a nitroso group (-NO) to form p-nitrosophenol.
p-nitrosophenol can exist in two tautomeric forms. In one form, it has a nitroso group (-NO) attached to the benzene ring, and in the other form, it becomes p-benzoquinone monoxime by the migration of a hydrogen atom and rearrangement of electrons within the molecule. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955044/preptracker/questions/q_28906_explanation_1764955043661.png So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Phenol is less acidic than p-nitrophenol.

Reason: Phenolate ion is more stable than $\mathrm{p-}$ nitrophenolate ion.
explanation: Phenol is less acidic than p-nitrophenol because the nitro group $\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)$ in p-nitrophenol is a strong electron-withdrawing group. This makes the phenolate ion (the conjugate base of phenol) less stable. In contrast, the p-nitrophenolate ion (the conjugate base of p- nitrophenol) is more stable due to the presence of the nitro group. The increased stability of the p-nitrophenolate ion results in greater acidity for p-nitrophenol compared to phenol. So the option (3) is correct.

Reason: The dipole moment of phenol is due to the electron withdrawing effect of phenyl group in contrast to the electron releasing effect of alkyl group in alcohols.
explanation: In phenol, the carbon atom is $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridized and due to this reason benzene ring is producing electron withdrawing effect. On the other hand,carbon of methanol is $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized and produces electron releasing effect ( $+\mathrm{I}$ effect). Thus, $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ bond in phenol is less polar than $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ bond in methanol. The dipole moment of phenol of $1.54 \mathrm{D}$. So the option (2) is correct.

question: Order of acidic nature
explanation:
Presence of electron withdrawing group (i.e. $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ ) increases the acidic nature in phenols.
$-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ at ortho - position [i.e. structure (B)] forms hydrogen bond with $-\mathrm{OH}$ group. Thus, structure (B) is less acidic than structure (A). On the other hand, electron releasing group bonded with the ring (i.e. $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ ) decreases the acidic nature, thus structure (C) is less acidic than phenol.
Hence, correct order of acidic nature is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949200/preptracker/questions/q_28502_explanation_1764949199820.png and option (2) is the correct

question:
Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their acidic strength
(i) m-Nitrophenol
(ii) $\mathrm{m}$-Cresol
(iii) Phenol
(iv) $\mathrm{m}$-Chlorophenol
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949135/preptracker/questions/q_28386_explanation_1764949135317.png   Nitro group has both -R effect and -I effect, but $-R$ effect predominates. Due to stronger electron withdrawing
nature of $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group, phenoxide ion is stablized more. Hence nitrophenol is more acidic than phenol.
Methyl group destabilizes the phenoxide ion by $+i$ effect and hyperconjugation.
Hence m-cresol is weaker acid than phenol.
Chlorine have both $+\mathrm{R}$ and $-\mathrm{I}$ effect but $-\mathrm{I}$ effect predominates. Hence $\mathrm{m}$-chlorophenol is more acidic than phenol.
- $\mathrm{R}$ effect of nitro group is stronger than -I effect of chlorine, hence m- nitrophenol is more acidic than $\mathrm{m}$ - chlorophenol. Therefore the correct order of acidic strength is
$\mathop {{\text{m - nitrophenol}} > }\limits_{{\text{(i)}}} \,\mathop {{\text{m - chlorophenol}} > }\limits_{{\text{(iv)}}} \,\mathop {{\text{phenol}} > \,}\limits_{{\text{(iii)}}} \mathop {{\text{m - cresol}}}\limits_{{\text{(ii)}}} $

question:
Assertion: Alcoholic fermentation involves conversion of sugar into ethyl alcohol by yeast.

Reason: Fermentation involves the slow decomposition of complex organic compounds into simpler substances through the agency of complex nitrogenous compounds called enzymes.
explanation:
Ethanol, $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$, is obtained commercially by fermentation. The sugar in molasses, sugarcane or fruits such as grapes is converted to glucose and fructose, (both of which have the formula ${{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_{12}}{{\text{O}}_6})$, in the presence of an enzyme, invertase. Glucose and fructose undergo fermentation in the presence of another enzyme, zymase, which is found in yeast.
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\text { invertase } \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6} \\& \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}(\mathrm{aq})+\text { zymase } \rightarrow \\& 2 \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}_{(\mathrm{aq})}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}\end{aligned}$
So the option (1) is correct

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

In ethers, the oxygen atom is $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridized.
The bond angle around the oxygen atom in ethers is slightly greater than the tetrahedral angle due to the repulsion between the lone pairs of electrons on oxygen atom.
explanation: In ethers, the oxygen atom is $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized, not $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$, because it is bonded to two carbon atoms with two sigma bonds and has two lone pairs of electrons. This makes Statement A false. Statement B is also false because the bond angle around the oxygen atom in ethers is slightly greater than the tetrahedral angle, which is dueto the repulsion between the two alkyl groups bonded to the oxygen atom. This repulsion causes a larger bond angle compared to a simple tetrahedral, which would have bond angles of $109.5^{\circ}$. Correct option is (4)

Reason: This sigma bond results in the bond angle in alcohols being slightly less than the tetrahedral angle due to the repulsion between the lone pairs of electrons on the oxygen atom.
explanation: The Assertion is true as the oxygen in the hydroxyl $(-\mathrm{OH})$ group of alcohols is indeed $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized and forms a sigma bond with the carbon. The Reason is also correct, as the bond angle ( $109^{\circ} \mathrm{S}$ ') is slightly less than the perfect tetrahedral angle ( $109^{\circ} 28^{\prime}$ ) because of the repulsion between the two lone pairs of electrons on the oxygen atom, which slightly compresses the angle between the bonds. Reason: Lone pair-lone pair repulsion decreases bond angle.
explanation: The bond angle in a molecule or ion can be less than the normal tetrahedral bond angle (approximately $109.5^{\circ}$ ) due to a phenomenon known as distortion or bending. In the given structure, which is of alcohols, there are two lone pairs on central oxygen that repel each other. This reduces the bond angle to $108.9^{\circ}$. So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Grignard reagents are prepared in ethers but not in benzene.


Reason: Grignard reagents are soluble in benzene.
explanation: The solubility of Grignard reagents in anhydrous ether is crucial for their reactivity, ensuring a water-free environment, easy preparation, and stable reaction conditions in organic synthesis. So the option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Grignard reagents are alkyl or aryl magnesium halides.


Reason: Alcohols can be used as solvent for Grignard reagent.
explanation: Alcohols are unsuitable solvents for Grignard reagents due to their reactivity, as the acidic hydrogen in alcohols can undergo undesired side reactions with Grignard reagents, leading to hydrocarbon formation and reduced reagent effectiveness. So the option (3) is correct.

explanation: Natalite is a trade name for the mixture of diethyl ether and ethyl alcohol. It is used as petrol substitute. It consist of 54 percent alcohol, 45 percent ether and 1 percent trimethylamine.
